title: VPN Split Tunneling Explained: Route Only What You Need
description: Split tunneling lets you choose which traffic goes through the VPN and which uses your regular connection. Here is why it matters and how to configure it.
content:
What Split Tunneling Does

Split tunneling divides your internet traffic into two paths: traffic you specify goes through the encrypted VPN tunnel, and the rest goes directly through your normal connection. Without split tunneling, all traffic routes through the VPN. This creates trade-offs: full VPN coverage means full privacy but lower speeds and potential issues with services that block VPN IPs (banking apps, some streaming services).

Two Types of Split Tunneling

App-based split tunneling lets you specify which applications use the VPN. You might route your torrent client and browser through the VPN while your Spotify and video calls go directly, preserving bandwidth and latency for real-time audio. URL-based split tunneling (less common) lets you route traffic to specific domains through the VPN only, keeping local services and banking apps on the regular connection.

When to Use It

Split tunneling is useful when you need VPN protection for specific activity but not for everything. Working from home with a corporate VPN: split-tunnel the work tools through the VPN and leave personal browsing local. Streaming geo-restricted content: route only the streaming app through the VPN so other traffic maintains full speed. Accessing local network devices (printers, NAS drives): exclude local subnet traffic so they remain reachable while the VPN is active.
